title: Quiche Lorraine
subtitle: French bacon and cream tart
excerpt: A deep shortcrust pastry shell filled with lardons, Gruyère, and a cream-and-egg custard that sets to a just-wobbling, barely-solid finish. The original — no broccoli, no sundried tomato.
sourceNotes: Quiche Lorraine from the Lorraine region of northeastern France. Documented in Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, and Simone Beck, Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961) and Elizabeth David, French Provincial Cooking (1960). The original version had no cheese — cheese was added by later cooks.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze whole or sliced for up to 2 months. Reheat from frozen in a low oven.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the day before; the custard sets further overnight and slices cleanly.

content.text: A proper quiche Lorraine has a custard that barely sets — it should wobble gently at the centre when you take it from the oven. It will continue to set as it cools. The enemy is over-baking: a quiche with a firm, uniform custard has been in the oven 10 minutes too long.

Use ready-made shortcrust pastry if you prefer — there is no shame in it and it gives a good result. If making your own, the pastry needs 30 minutes' rest in the fridge after making and before blind-baking.

items.prepNote: ready-made, or make with 200 g plain flour + 100 g cold unsalted butter + 3 tbsp cold water

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Roll the pastry to line a 23 cm loose-bottomed tart tin, pressing into the edges. Trim the overhang. Refrigerate for 30 minutes. Line with baking paper, fill with baking beans, and blind-bake for 15 minutes. Remove the paper and beans and bake for a further 5 minutes until the base looks dry. Let it cool slightly.

Fry the lardons in a dry frying pan over medium heat until the fat renders and they are lightly golden. Drain and scatter over the pastry base. Scatter the Gruyère over the lardons. Whisk the eggs, cream, milk, nutmeg, salt, and pepper together. Pour carefully into the pastry shell — fill to just below the rim. Bake for 28 to 32 minutes until the custard is just set at the edges but still has a slight wobble at the centre. Rest for 10 minutes before slicing.

items.cause: Not blind-baked long enough, or baked on a cold tray
items.fix: Blind-bake until the base looks thoroughly dry, not just set; place the tart tin on a preheated baking tray to help the base cook from below
Check the temperature and bake at exactly 180°C fan; remove when just-wobbling — it will set further off the heat

content.text: Quiche Lorraine has been made in Lorraine, a region near the German border that was historically part of both France and Germany, for centuries. The name quiche derives from the German Kuchen (cake). The dish became internationally famous after the Second World War as French cuisine spread globally. In the 1970s it was embraced by British and American cooks as a lunch-party standard, then fell slightly from favour. It deserves its rehabilitation.
